abstract: In this study, a cerium-based metal–organic framework (Ce-MOF) was synthesized through a hydrothermal method using a 4,4′,4′′-nitrilotribenzoic acid linker. The resulting framework features a combination of Lewis acidic Ce³⁺ centers and triphenylamine cores, endowing the material with robust Fenton-like catalytic properties. Ce-MOF demonstrated exceptional efficiency in the oxidation of olefins, as well as primary and secondary alcohols. Specifically, the catalyst achieved a 100% conversion rate for cinnamyl alcohol and a 53% conversion for styrene, with high selectivity toward benzaldehyde (100%) and styrene oxide (75%), respectively. Beyond fine chemical synthesis, the Ce-MOF was utilized for the advanced oxidative degradation of organic water pollutants, including Rhodamine B (RhB), Methyl Blue (MeB), Congo Red (CR), and Direct Blue (DB), using hydrogen peroxide as an oxidant. Mechanistic investigations confirmed that the catalytic process relies on the generation of reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH). Overall, this work highlights the potential of Ce-MOFs to enhance advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) for environmental remediation. Importantly, the catalyst exhibited excellent stability and recyclability, maintaining its performance over five consecutive cycles.
